findings.title: Cascade: caller's own workspace is included in the distribution it is being ranked against, biasing percentile and milestone tier when the caller has been inactive >=14d
findings.evidence: Trigger: caller-workspace W has lastOrderAt older than now - 14d (e.g., a workspace that briefly tested last week of last festival and now opens dashboard 14+ days later). Path: SeasonInsightService.getInsight calls aggregateForWorkspace(W) producing aggregate A, AND distributionService.computeDistribution() which calls repo.aggregateAll() and filters `lastOrderAt.isBefore(cutoff)` — this filter does not exclude W. So distribution.totalSales / avgOrderAmount / etc contain W's own metrics. Then StrengthSelector and MilestoneTierResolver call PercentileCalculator.percentile(value=A.metric, distribution=[..., A.metric, ...]). In PercentileCalculator.kt:12 `count { it > value }` excludes the equal self-entry, but the population total still counts the self-entry. Concrete consequence: a workspace whose totalSales is the unique max in a population of 5 ended workspaces produces strictlyBetter=0, rank=1, percentile=ceil(1/5*100)=20, so MilestoneTier=TOP_30 — but the 'true' peer ranking should exclude self (rank 1 of 4 peers = ceil(1/4*100)=25, still TOP_30 here, but the population size near MIN_POPULATION_SIZE=5 makes this an off-by-one that can flip a tier at the boundary). More subtle: when values equal one of the peer values (a tie at the top), self-inclusion inflates the denominator, deflating the user's apparent rank percentile (lower=better here), giving a falsely better tier. Outcome: misleading milestone advertised to user, contradicting stated 'compared against peers' semantics.

findings.title: Composition: active in-progress caller is compared against fully-completed (>=14d ended) workspaces, producing systematically deflated milestone tier mid-season
findings.evidence: Trigger: caller workspace W is mid-festival (e.g., day 2 of a 5-day event, lastOrderAt = today). aggregateForWorkspace(W) returns W.totalSales = (2 days of revenue, e.g., 800k). computeDistribution() returns ended workspaces (lastOrderAt < now - 14d), each containing FULL season totals (e.g., 5M-30M won). MilestoneTierResolver.resolve(800_000, dist) -> 800_000 < MILESTONE_FLOOR_KRW=1_000_000 returns null. As soon as W passes 1M (mid-day-3), percentileLong is invoked: strictlyBetter ~= populationSize, rank ~= populationSize, percentile -> 100, fromPercentile(100)=null. So user perpetually sees no tier until literally end-of-season — but this is dressed up as 'rank vs peers'. Strengths similarly: avgOrderAmount and lowCancellationRate are scale-invariant so they may give a meaningful percentile, but turnoverRate=orders/businessDayCount/tableCount and tableRevenue=totalSales/tableCount are partially time-cumulative (numerator grows with days). Comparing a mid-season caller's tableRevenue against full-season peers => low percentile => 'strength' badge gets falsely awarded or denied based on how much of the season the user has played, not on their actual performance. Composition failure: distribution semantics ('completed seasons') ≠ caller semantics ('all-time accumulator including in-progress'). No marker in the response tells the frontend the comparison may be invalid mid-season.

findings.title: Cascade: SocialProof advertises false 0원 'top performer' when all ended workspaces have zero revenue, misleading active operators
findings.evidence: Trigger: ≥5 workspaces have rows with lastOrderAt older than 14d (so populationSize ≥ MIN_POPULATION_SIZE=5), but all of them have only cancelled orders OR zero totalPrice (e.g., test fixtures, abandoned demo workspaces). Path: SeasonDistributionService.kt:46 builds totalSales = ended.map{ it.totalSales }.sorted() => [0, 0, 0, 0, 0]. populationSize = 5 (NOT 0, so guard at SeasonInsightService.kt:41 `if (dist.populationSize == 0)` is bypassed). Line 42: `dist.totalSales.maxOrNull() ?: 0L` returns 0L, floorToManwon(0L)=0L. Same for topAvgOrder and topTurnover. Outcome: API returns SocialProofDto(topSales=0, topAvgOrderAmount=0, topTurnoverRate=0.0). Frontend will render 'Top performer: 0원' as social proof — actively misleading and embarrassing UX. Same scenario distorts MilestoneTierResolver: any caller with totalSales >= 1M won (the floor) but compared against [0,0,0,0,0] gets percentile=ceil(1/5*100)=20 -> TOP_30; even percentile semantics break if all peers have lowCancellationRate=null (filtered) and StrengthSelector calls percentile(v, [], true) which returns 100 (treated as worst), suppressing strength surfaces unexpectedly.

findings.title: Assumption violation: PercentileCalculator returns percentile=100 ('worst') for empty distribution dimensions, conflating 'no data' with 'bad rank'
findings.evidence: Trigger: distribution has populationSize >= 5 but for a specific metric all members are null after mapNotNull (e.g., every ended workspace has tableCount=0, so SeasonMetricsCalculator.kt:14 yields turnoverRate=null and tableRevenue=null; SeasonDistributionService.kt:43-44 mapNotNull strips them, leaving empty list). Path: StrengthSelector.kt:12 sees populationSize>0 (truthy) and calls percentileCalc.percentile(v, distribution.turnoverRate=[], true). PercentileCalculator.kt:9 returns 100 (the early-return for empty was meant to mean 'worst' or 'unknown'). StrengthSelector then filters `it.percentile <= 50` so this metric is never offered as a strength even when caller actually performs well. Worse for MilestoneTierResolver: it uses distribution.totalSales which is `ended.map { it.totalSales }` (no mapNotNull), so cannot be empty unless populationSize=0 — that path is guarded — but the inconsistency between 'populationSize gate' and 'per-metric empty list' propagates wrong semantics into the UI.

findings.title: Composition: PercentileCalculator returns inflated rank for ties in the distribution, making average performers appear top-quintile
findings.evidence: Trigger: distribution = [5.0, 5.0, 5.0, 5.0, 5.0], caller value = 5.0 (ties at median). Path: PercentileCalculator.kt:11-12 `distribution.count { it > 5.0 }` = 0. rank = (0+1).coerceIn(1,5) = 1. percentile = ceil(1/5*100) = 20. Result: caller appears 'TOP_30 percentile / strength worthy', triggering MilestoneTier.TOP_30 and StrengthSelector adding the metric to the strengths list (since 20 <= 50). Realistic scenario: many small workspaces have similar avg order amounts (e.g., everybody sells 5,000원 drinks) — every one of them sees themselves as 'top 20%' for that metric. The promise of 'objective ranking against peers' degenerates into universal flattery. Same logic with biggerIsBetter=false gives identical inflation. The standard mid-rank for ties (or 'fractional rank') is not used.

findings.title: Abuse case: workspace MEMBER (not just OWNER) can read aggregate competitor stats via SocialProof
findings.evidence: Trigger: legitimate user holds membership in workspace W (added as a part-time staff). Path: facade calls workspaceService.checkAccessible(username, W) -> WorkspaceService.kt:85-94 isAccessible returns true if workspaceMemberRepository.existsByWorkspaceIdAndUserLoginId(W, username) — i.e., ANY membership row, not just owner. Response includes SocialProofDto with topSales, topAvgOrderAmount, topTurnoverRate aggregated across all ended workspaces in the system. Outcome: a part-time staff member of any participating workspace can poll /admin/workspaces/{W}/season-insight and read aggregate competitive intelligence (top performer totals across all schools/festivals). Whether this is intended depends on product policy, but the PR intent says 'admin' — implying owner-tier access, not staff-tier. If unintended, this is a data exposure regression: the existing /admin endpoints largely guard against members, but season insight piggybacks on the more permissive checkAccessible path.

findings.title: Cascade: 14-day inactivity threshold + KST clock + DB-side date casting creates jitter at midnight boundary; cache freezes the wrong distribution for up to 1 hour
findings.evidence: Trigger: workspace W's lastOrderAt = 2026-04-12T14:00 KST. computeDistribution runs at 2026-04-26T14:00 KST: cutoff = 2026-04-12T14:00; W.isBefore(cutoff)=false, W not included. At 2026-04-26T14:01: cutoff = 2026-04-12T14:01; W.isBefore=true, W included. The result is cached for 1h with key 'global'. So whichever request happens first within the hour decides whether the just-crossed-boundary workspaces are in the distribution for the next hour. Concurrent path: between request A (which excludes W) caching for 1h and a later distribution build request B (which would include W), B reads stale cache that lacks W. This skews populationSize (e.g., right at MIN_POPULATION_SIZE=5 boundary — including/excluding W can flip the entire distribution between 'empty result' and 'real result'). Combined with active-vs-ended composition above, downstream caller sees milestone flip TOP_30 -> null with no apparent cause. No vary-by-time bucket; cache key is constant 'global'. Lower confidence because exact production timing impact depends on traffic shape.

findings.title: Cascade: cache stampede on first request after eviction recomputes aggregateAll() concurrently because RedisCacheWriter is non-locking
findings.evidence: Trigger: cache 'seasonDistribution#1h' expires (or process restart, or admin-initiated flush). Multiple admin browsers polling season-insight simultaneously. Path: CacheConfiguration.kt:95 uses RedisCacheWriter.nonLockingRedisCacheWriter. Each concurrent request misses, each invokes SeasonInsightService.getInsight -> distributionService.computeDistribution -> repo.aggregateAll(): full-table aggregation across ALL workspaces and ALL orders (no time bound) with groupBy and DISTINCT date count — an expensive query. N concurrent requests => N concurrent expensive queries on the DB at the moment of expiry. With polling-based UI (intent says 'polling'), every 5m (seasonInsight cache TTL) every active dashboard creates its own seasonInsight cache miss; on each 1h boundary, every active dashboard ALSO triggers a distribution recomputation. Outcome: predictable load spikes on the hour, potentially saturating DB connections, cascading into other tenants' degraded latency. No per-key locking, no early refresh.

findings.title: Composition: aggregateAll() unbounded scan over entire orders table without time filter; degrades over multi-year retention
findings.evidence: Trigger: deployment continues past several festival seasons; orders table accumulates years of rows. Path: aggregateAll() (line 64-110) does `from(workspace).leftJoin(order).on(order.workspace.id.eq(workspace.id)).groupBy(workspace.id, workspace.tableCount)` with no WHERE clause limiting the time window. Computes totalSales / totalOrders / cancelledOrders / businessDayCount / lastOrderAt over ALL historical orders. Combined with SeasonDistributionService.kt:27 filter `lastOrderAt.isBefore(cutoff)` — workspaces inactive for years are STILL included as 'ended' members of the distribution. Outcome 1: distribution.totalSales contains lifetime totals from years-old workspaces, dominating the percentile and inflating SocialProofDto.topSales to a multi-year cumulative figure presented as if it were a single 'season' top — false advertising. Outcome 2: the leftJoin scans the full orders fact table for every cache miss (each hour); on a multi-year DB this becomes a multi-second query, exacerbating cache stampede above. The 'season' concept is undefined in code: there is no season start/end column anywhere; ended-heuristic only checks recency.

findings.title: Assumption violation: businessDayCount uses DB-side cast(createdAt as date) but JVM clock is KST — day boundaries diverge if DB session timezone is not Asia/Seoul
findings.evidence: Trigger: festival workspace runs 18:00 KST to 02:00 KST next day for two nights. Path: SeasonInsightRepository.kt:42 `count(distinct cast({0} as date))` is rendered to SQL DATE() / cast which uses the database session timezone. ClockConfiguration.kt:11 sets JVM clock to Asia/Seoul, but order.createdAt is typically stored as TIMESTAMP (UTC or session TZ). If DB session TZ is UTC: orders at 23:00 KST and 01:00 KST same KST-day cast as different UTC dates; orders at 08:00 KST and 09:00 KST same UTC-date may or may not collapse. Outcome: businessDayCount computed by repository diverges from a KST-day-of-business count, so turnoverRate=orders/businessDayCount/tableCount is systematically biased (often overcounts days, deflating turnoverRate). Same caller-vs-distribution-comparison-validity concern. Lower confidence because actual DB session TZ in production is not visible from the diff.

findings.title: Composition: SocialProof topAvgOrderAmount uses (Double).toLong() truncation that silently drops sub-won precision and can overflow on extreme values
findings.evidence: Trigger: any single ended workspace with avgOrderAmount > Long.MAX_VALUE would overflow on toLong(); not realistic in won. More realistic: avgOrderAmount=12345.67 (Double) -> toLong()=12345 -> floorToManwon=10000. The shown 'top average order' is then ≤ what was internally computed. Composition issue: SeasonMetricsCalculator.kt:12 returns avgOrderAmount as Double (precision suitable for percentile math), but SocialProof presents it as Long won. The conversion happens at presentation, so internal percentile math is correct, but the displayed value can be off by up to ~1만원 due to floorToManwon applied AFTER toLong truncation. If the displayed top is presented as 'top performer earned X원/order', actual top performer earned X+ε원, X+ε can be in a different 만원 bucket — small UX inconsistency, possibly more impactful if shown next to a leaderboard.

findings.title: Abuse case: cache-key uses Long workspaceId without validation; SUPER_ADMIN polling many random workspaceIds inflates Redis memory and hides authorization issues
findings.evidence: Trigger: a SUPER_ADMIN account (or any account that bypasses checkAccessible) iterates GET /admin/workspaces/{i}/season-insight for i=1..100000. Path: WorkspaceService.kt:91 short-circuits accessibility for SUPER_ADMIN; SeasonInsightService.kt:21 caches by workspaceId regardless of whether the workspace exists. SeasonInsightRepository.kt:15 returns a zero-aggregate for missing workspaces (tableCount=0, tuple=null path). Each unique id allocates a Redis cache entry for 5 minutes -> up to ~100k cache entries with no eviction policy from this code. No upper bound or existence check before caching. Outcome: Redis memory growth, possible eviction of more important caches; also obscures the fact that querying a non-existent workspaceId returns a successful 200 response with empty data instead of 404, which complicates client error handling and audit trails.

residual_risks: The 'season' concept is implicit (recency-based heuristic) rather than explicit (no Season entity / start-end columns). Any interpretation of 'compared to peers in this season' is approximate; future seasons may need explicit modelling to avoid the active-vs-ended composition mismatch.

Comparison fairness across heterogeneous workspace sizes (10-table cafe vs 200-table festival site) is not normalized beyond per-table metrics — a very large workspace can dominate totalSales-based MilestoneTier irrespective of efficiency; consider whether milestone tier should bucket by tableCount.

Polling-based UX combined with 5m insight cache and 1h distribution cache means every dashboard sees up to 1 hour of staleness and refresh is bursty. Background refresh (scheduled job populating cache on a fixed cadence) would reduce stampede risk but is not implemented.
testing_gaps: No test where caller's own workspace is itself in the 'ended' set used by computeDistribution (self-inclusion bias).
No test where all ended workspaces have totalSales=0 / all have tableCount=0 (false 0원 social proof, percentile=100 for empty per-metric distribution).
No test for tied distribution values producing inflated percentile (e.g., distribution=[5,5,5,5,5], value=5).
No test exercising MEMBER (non-owner, non-super-admin) access through SeasonInsightFacade — confirms whether members should see SocialProof.
No test for caller with active mid-season workspace vs ended-season distribution (composition mismatch).
No test for repository behavior when DB session timezone differs from KST (businessDayCount drift).
No concurrency / cache stampede test (multiple simultaneous misses).
No test for non-existent workspaceId reaching SeasonInsightService (returns 200 with empty insight).
